title: Postpartum Haemorrhage
oneLiner: Heavy bleeding (>500ml vaginal or >1000ml caesarean) within 24h (primary) or 24h-12w (secondary); leading cause maternal death globally.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive haemorrhage with hypovolaemic shock:** Look for tachycardia >110, hypotension, altered mentation, oliguria — *bleeding may be underestimated; treat the physiology not the volume.*
- **Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE):** Sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, DIC during labour or within 30 min of delivery — *no fever, no clear septic focus; Look for acute right heart strain (cyanosis, raised JVP, hypoxia out of proportion) + unexplained coagulopathy.*
- **Uterine inversion:** Acute severe pain, shock out of proportion to visible blood loss, a mass at/introitus or absent fundus on palpation — *do not try to remove placenta before confirming fundus position.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible vaginal bleeding – may be continuous trickle or torrential gush; can be concealed if uterus atonic (bleeding into cavity)
  - Uterine atony – fundus soft, boggy, palpable above umbilicus
  - Clots per vaginam
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, lightheadedness
  - Secondary PPH: offensive lochia, fever, abdominal pain (infection/retained products)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpate fundus: height, tone (muscle hard = good, boggy = atonic)
  - Inspect perineum/vagina/cervix for lacerations (speculum examination after placental delivery if bleeding persists despite contracted uterus)
  - Check for vulvar haematomas (tense, painful, often with minimal visible bleeding)
  - Evidence of shock: CRT >2s, BP drop, narrow pulse pressure
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Uterine atony** (70%) → boggy fundus, responds to bimanual compression; bleeding dark, continuous
- **Genital tract trauma** → sharp laceration, bright red blood, fundus firm; associated with forceps/ventouse or large baby
- **Retained placenta / products** → placenta incomplete on inspection, bleeding starts after placenta delivery; ultrasound shows echogenic mass
- **Coagulopathy** → bleeding from puncture sites, mucosal oozing, known coagulopathy (HELLP, abruption, DIC)
- **Uterine rupture** → fetal distress, abdominal pain, loss of station (rare; usually previous CS or trauma)
- **Placenta accreta** → difficult placental separation, heavy bleeding (known placenta praevia / prior CS)
- **Splitter:** **Firm fundus + persistent bright red bleeding** → likely trauma (laceration), treat with suturing not uterotonics.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Vital signs (RR, HR, BP, SpO2, urine output)
  - Visual estimation of blood loss (use collection drape, weigh swabs)
  - Bimanual palpation of fundal tone
  - Speculum exam to identify lacerations
  - If suspected retained products: urgent bedside ultrasound (if skilled) → retained tissue vs empty cavity
  - Point-of-care Hb (Hb may be falsely normal early)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen), group & save / crossmatch (minimum 4 units)
  - U+E, LFTs (HELLP workup)
  - Blood gas for lactate and base deficit
  - Kleihauer (if RhD negative) – for anti-D dose
- **Imaging:**
  - Urgent pelvic ultrasound (transabdominal) once stable – assess retained products, haematometra, intra-abdominal free fluid
  - CT abdomen/pelvis if suspected uterine rupture or intra-abdominal bleed (unstable → go to theatre)
  - When stable, CT angiogram for arteriovenous fistula or pseudoaneurysm (rare)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - WHO/ROOG definitions: Primary PPH (delivery to 24h) – minor (500-1000ml), major (>1000ml), massive (>2000ml or need for ≥4 units blood)
  - Shock Index (HR/SBP): >0.9 raises concern; >1.5 indicates massive transfusion need
  - Modified Obstetric Early Warning Score (MEOWS) / NEWS2 triggers: call senior if HR >110, SBP <90, RR >25, SpO2 <94%
- **Never-miss test:** **Group & crossmatch** – in any PPH >500ml or haemodynamically unstable; send immediately. Do not wait for Hb result.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / delivery suite settings):**
  - **Call for help:** Midwife, obstetrician, anaesthetist, haematology (Massive Transfusion Protocol if required)
  - **ABC:** High-flow oxygen, IV access ×2 (14G grey cannulae), bloods as above, start fluid resuscitation (1L warmed crystalloid fast) but shift to blood products early
  - **Uterine atony** (most common): bimanual compression + uterotonics
  - **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 5 IU IV slow bolus (over 1 min) then infusion (40 IU in 500mL 0.9% saline over 4h)
  - **Ergometrine** 0.5mg IM (contraindicated if hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease)
  - **Carboprost (Hemabate)** 0.25mg IM every 15 min (max 8 doses) – for atony refractory; caution if asthma
  - **Misoprostol** 400-800mcg sublingual/rectal if IV access lost (no license, but commonly used)
  - **Mechanical:** Intrauterine balloon (e.g. Bakri) – inflate with 200-500mL sterile water; if fails, consider uterine compression sutures / hysterectomy
  - **If trauma:** Repair laceration (continuous locked suture), if haematoma – evacuate if expanding
  - **If retained placenta:** Manual removal (with adequate analgesia +/- sedation) or ultrasound-guided curettage
  - **Coagulopathy:** Give fresh frozen plasma (FFP) if PT/APTT >1.5, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <2g/L, platelets if <50×10⁹/L
  - **Massive transfusion:** Red cells:FFP:platelets in 1:1:1 ratio; use cell salvage if available
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor vital signs and urine output hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly if stable
  - Check FBC, coagulation 4-6h post event; repeat as needed
  - Continue oxytocin infusion until bleeding controlled (usually 4h)
  - Iron infusion or IV iron if Hb <70g/L (after stabilisation)
  - Prophylactic antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or cefuroxime + metronidazole) if manual removal or intrauterine manipulation
  - Thromboprophylaxis (enoxaparin) once bleeding stopped and no ongoing coagulopathy – start after 24h if no bleeding
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Repeat Hb and ferritin at 6 weeks; treat anaemia (oral iron)
  - Screen for postpartum psychological trauma (debrief, signpost to perinatal mental health)
  - Future pregnancy planning: risk of recurrence (especially placental pathology, accreta)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride – 80-100mL/h until oral intake tolerated (avoid dextrose initially)
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjusted for weight/renal function) – start only when bleeding controlled and no coagulopathy
  - Diet: NBM initially if likely need theatre; light diet once stable and no ongoing bleed
  - Observations: NEWS2/MEOWS – hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly for 24h if stable; escalate if HR>100, BP<90, urine output <0.5mL/kg/h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 5 IU slow IV bolus (over 1 min) then infusion 40 IU in 500mL 0.9% saline at 125mL/h for 4h. Repeat bolus if ongoing atony.
- **Ergometrine:** 0.5mg IM (can be combined with oxytocin as Syntometrine). Contraindicated: pre-eclampsia, hypertension, ischaemic heart disease.
- **Carboprost (Prostin 15M, Hemabate):** 0.25mg deep IM every 15 min, max 2mg (8 doses). Caution: asthma, pulmonary hypertension. Side effects: vomiting, diarrhoea, bronchospasm.
- **Misoprostol:** 400-800mcg sublingual, oral, or rectal (unlicensed but widely used). Do not exceed total of 1200mcg in 24h.
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 min (WOMAN trial); repeat after 30 min if bleeding continues. Contraindicated if thromboembolism in progress.
- **Calcium gluconate** (if massive transfusion): 10mL of 10% solution IV (over 10 min) for hypocalcaemia (citrate toxicity)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ergometrine is renally cleared; reduce in severe impairment. Oxytocin – no adjustment. Misoprostol – no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxytocin bolus too fast** (>5 IU IV push) → severe hypotension, tachycardia, myocardial ischaemia. Always give over 1 minute and only as bolus if needed; prefer infusion.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock and death (leading cause)
  - DIC – consumptive coagulopathy, often from massive transfusion or sepsis
  - Transfusion reactions, transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
  - Acute kidney injury (from hypoperfusion)
  - Sheehan’s syndrome (pituitary necrosis) – rare but life-long; suspect if failure to lactate + secondary amenorrhoea
  - Uterine inversion, endometritis, wound infection
- **Chronic:**
  - Postpartum anaemia (iron deficiency)
  - Psychological trauma: PTSD, depression, avoidance of future pregnancy
  - Recurrence risk: increased for placenta praevia, accreta, atony
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality ~1-3 per 100,000 deliveries in UK/Ireland; higher with delayed recognition. Most survive with rapid, correct management. Long-term morbidity: chronic anaemia, psychological sequelae. Risk of recurrence: 5-10% (higher if cause is uterine atony or retained placenta).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Palpate the fundus immediately in any postpartum bleed** – if boggy, bimanual compression + oxytocin. This buys time, often works.
- **Order tranexamic acid within 30 minutes** – reduces mortality if given early; one dose is cheap and safe.
- **Use shock index (HR/SBP) not just BP** – a young woman can have SBP 100 until collapse; shock index >0.9 is early red flag.
- **Check fibrinogen in massive haemorrhage** – if <2g/L, give cryoprecipitate (or FFP if unavailable).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not wait for Hb to drop before giving blood** – Hb falls slowly; treat with blood products based on clinical shock and ongoing risk.
- **Do not give ergometrine if BP is >150/100** – risk of hypertensive crisis and stroke.
- **Do not assume secondary PPH is “just bleeding”** – always examine for infection/retained products; treat early with antibiotics and ultrasound.
- **Do not underestimate blood loss** – swabs and pads can lose 500mL silently; use a gravity collection bag and weigh swabs.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New heavy bleeding (soaking pad in <1h), fever, rigors, abdominal pain, passing large clots, feeling faint or dizzy.
- **Follow-up:** GP – review Hb in 1-2 weeks, ferritin at 6 weeks; offer debrief with community midwife or consultant at 6-8 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (obstetric registrar / consultant):**
  - Any ongoing bleeding >1000mL despite initial uterotonics
  - Haemodynamic instability (HR>120, SBP<90, shock index >1)
  - Abnormal clotting (PT/APTT >1.5× control, fibrinogen <2g/L)
  - Suspected uterine rupture or inversion
  - Criteria for massive transfusion (active bleeding + shock)
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - GCS <13, respiratory distress, SpO2 <90% on high-flow O2
  - Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest (obstetric cardiac arrest drill – left lateral tilt, prepare for perimortem CS)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2: >6 (or any single parameter red) → immediate clinical review
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Urine output <0.5mL/kg/h for >2h despite fluid resuscitation

rawMarkdown:
## Postpartum Haemorrhage
**One-liner:** Heavy bleeding (>500ml vaginal or >1000ml caesarean) within 24h (primary) or 24h-12w (secondary); leading cause maternal death globally.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive haemorrhage with hypovolaemic shock:** Look for tachycardia >110, hypotension, altered mentation, oliguria — *bleeding may be underestimated; treat the physiology not the volume.*
- **Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE):** Sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, DIC during labour or within 30 min of delivery — *no fever, no clear septic focus; Look for acute right heart strain (cyanosis, raised JVP, hypoxia out of proportion) + unexplained coagulopathy.*
- **Uterine inversion:** Acute severe pain, shock out of proportion to visible blood loss, a mass at/introitus or absent fundus on palpation — *do not try to remove placenta before confirming fundus position.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible vaginal bleeding – may be continuous trickle or torrential gush; can be concealed if uterus atonic (bleeding into cavity)
  - Uterine atony – fundus soft, boggy, palpable above umbilicus
  - Clots per vaginam
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, lightheadedness
  - Secondary PPH: offensive lochia, fever, abdominal pain (infection/retained products)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpate fundus: height, tone (muscle hard = good, boggy = atonic)
  - Inspect perineum/vagina/cervix for lacerations (speculum examination after placental delivery if bleeding persists despite contracted uterus)
  - Check for vulvar haematomas (tense, painful, often with minimal visible bleeding)
  - Evidence of shock: CRT >2s, BP drop, narrow pulse pressure

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Uterine atony** (70%) → boggy fundus, responds to bimanual compression; bleeding dark, continuous
- **Genital tract trauma** → sharp laceration, bright red blood, fundus firm; associated with forceps/ventouse or large baby
- **Retained placenta / products** → placenta incomplete on inspection, bleeding starts after placenta delivery; ultrasound shows echogenic mass
- **Coagulopathy** → bleeding from puncture sites, mucosal oozing, known coagulopathy (HELLP, abruption, DIC)
- **Uterine rupture** → fetal distress, abdominal pain, loss of station (rare; usually previous CS or trauma)
- **Placenta accreta** → difficult placental separation, heavy bleeding (known placenta praevia / prior CS)
- **Splitter:** **Firm fundus + persistent bright red bleeding** → likely trauma (laceration), treat with suturing not uterotonics.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Vital signs (RR, HR, BP, SpO2, urine output)
  - Visual estimation of blood loss (use collection drape, weigh swabs)
  - Bimanual palpation of fundal tone
  - Speculum exam to identify lacerations
  - If suspected retained products: urgent bedside ultrasound (if skilled) → retained tissue vs empty cavity
  - Point-of-care Hb (Hb may be falsely normal early)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen), group & save / crossmatch (minimum 4 units)
  - U+E, LFTs (HELLP workup)
  - Blood gas for lactate and base deficit
  - Kleihauer (if RhD negative) – for anti-D dose
- **Imaging:**
  - Urgent pelvic ultrasound (transabdominal) once stable – assess retained products, haematometra, intra-abdominal free fluid
  - CT abdomen/pelvis if suspected uterine rupture or intra-abdominal bleed (unstable → go to theatre)
  - When stable, CT angiogram for arteriovenous fistula or pseudoaneurysm (rare)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - WHO/ROOG definitions: Primary PPH (delivery to 24h) – minor (500-1000ml), major (>1000ml), massive (>2000ml or need for ≥4 units blood)
  - Shock Index (HR/SBP): >0.9 raises concern; >1.5 indicates massive transfusion need
  - Modified Obstetric Early Warning Score (MEOWS) / NEWS2 triggers: call senior if HR >110, SBP <90, RR >25, SpO2 <94%
- **Never-miss test:** **Group & crossmatch** – in any PPH >500ml or haemodynamically unstable; send immediately. Do not wait for Hb result.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / delivery suite settings):**
  - **Call for help:** Midwife, obstetrician, anaesthetist, haematology (Massive Transfusion Protocol if required)
  - **ABC:** High-flow oxygen, IV access ×2 (14G grey cannulae), bloods as above, start fluid resuscitation (1L warmed crystalloid fast) but shift to blood products early
  - **Uterine atony** (most common): bimanual compression + uterotonics
  - **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 5 IU IV slow bolus (over 1 min) then infusion (40 IU in 500mL 0.9% saline over 4h)
  - **Ergometrine** 0.5mg IM (contraindicated if hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease)
  - **Carboprost (Hemabate)** 0.25mg IM every 15 min (max 8 doses) – for atony refractory; caution if asthma
  - **Misoprostol** 400-800mcg sublingual/rectal if IV access lost (no license, but commonly used)
  - **Mechanical:** Intrauterine balloon (e.g. Bakri) – inflate with 200-500mL sterile water; if fails, consider uterine compression sutures / hysterectomy
  - **If trauma:** Repair laceration (continuous locked suture), if haematoma – evacuate if expanding
  - **If retained placenta:** Manual removal (with adequate analgesia +/- sedation) or ultrasound-guided curettage
  - **Coagulopathy:** Give fresh frozen plasma (FFP) if PT/APTT >1.5, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <2g/L, platelets if <50×10⁹/L
  - **Massive transfusion:** Red cells:FFP:platelets in 1:1:1 ratio; use cell salvage if available
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor vital signs and urine output hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly if stable
  - Check FBC, coagulation 4-6h post event; repeat as needed
  - Continue oxytocin infusion until bleeding controlled (usually 4h)
  - Iron infusion or IV iron if Hb <70g/L (after stabilisation)
  - Prophylactic antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or cefuroxime + metronidazole) if manual removal or intrauterine manipulation
  - Thromboprophylaxis (enoxaparin) once bleeding stopped and no ongoing coagulopathy – start after 24h if no bleeding
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Repeat Hb and ferritin at 6 weeks; treat anaemia (oral iron)
  - Screen for postpartum psychological trauma (debrief, signpost to perinatal mental health)
  - Future pregnancy planning: risk of recurrence (especially placental pathology, accreta)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride – 80-100mL/h until oral intake tolerated (avoid dextrose initially)
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjusted for weight/renal function) – start only when bleeding controlled and no coagulopathy
  - Diet: NBM initially if likely need theatre; light diet once stable and no ongoing bleed
  - Observations: NEWS2/MEOWS – hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly for 24h if stable; escalate if HR>100, BP<90, urine output <0.5mL/kg/h

### Drugs & Doses
- **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 5 IU slow IV bolus (over 1 min) then infusion 40 IU in 500mL 0.9% saline at 125mL/h for 4h. Repeat bolus if ongoing atony.
- **Ergometrine:** 0.5mg IM (can be combined with oxytocin as Syntometrine). Contraindicated: pre-eclampsia, hypertension, ischaemic heart disease.
- **Carboprost (Prostin 15M, Hemabate):** 0.25mg deep IM every 15 min, max 2mg (8 doses). Caution: asthma, pulmonary hypertension. Side effects: vomiting, diarrhoea, bronchospasm.
- **Misoprostol:** 400-800mcg sublingual, oral, or rectal (unlicensed but widely used). Do not exceed total of 1200mcg in 24h.
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 min (WOMAN trial); repeat after 30 min if bleeding continues. Contraindicated if thromboembolism in progress.
- **Calcium gluconate** (if massive transfusion): 10mL of 10% solution IV (over 10 min) for hypocalcaemia (citrate toxicity)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ergometrine is renally cleared; reduce in severe impairment. Oxytocin – no adjustment. Misoprostol – no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxytocin bolus too fast** (>5 IU IV push) → severe hypotension, tachycardia, myocardial ischaemia. Always give over 1 minute and only as bolus if needed; prefer infusion.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock and death (leading cause)
  - DIC – consumptive coagulopathy, often from massive transfusion or sepsis
  - Transfusion reactions, transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
  - Acute kidney injury (from hypoperfusion)
  - Sheehan’s syndrome (pituitary necrosis) – rare but life-long; suspect if failure to lactate + secondary amenorrhoea
  - Uterine inversion, endometritis, wound infection
- **Chronic:**
  - Postpartum anaemia (iron deficiency)
  - Psychological trauma: PTSD, depression, avoidance of future pregnancy
  - Recurrence risk: increased for placenta praevia, accreta, atony

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~1-3 per 100,000 deliveries in UK/Ireland; higher with delayed recognition. Most survive with rapid, correct management. Long-term morbidity: chronic anaemia, psychological sequelae. Risk of recurrence: 5-10% (higher if cause is uterine atony or retained placenta).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Palpate the fundus immediately in any postpartum bleed** – if boggy, bimanual compression + oxytocin. This buys time, often works.
- **Order tranexamic acid within 30 minutes** – reduces mortality if given early; one dose is cheap and safe.
- **Use shock index (HR/SBP) not just BP** – a young woman can have SBP 100 until collapse; shock index >0.9 is early red flag.
- **Check fibrinogen in massive haemorrhage** – if <2g/L, give cryoprecipitate (or FFP if unavailable).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not wait for Hb to drop before giving blood** – Hb falls slowly; treat with blood products based on clinical shock and ongoing risk.
- **Do not give ergometrine if BP is >150/100** – risk of hypertensive crisis and stroke.
- **Do not assume secondary PPH is “just bleeding”** – always examine for infection/retained products; treat early with antibiotics and ultrasound.
- **Do not underestimate blood loss** – swabs and pads can lose 500mL silently; use a gravity collection bag and weigh swabs.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New heavy bleeding (soaking pad in <1h), fever, rigors, abdominal pain, passing large clots, feeling faint or dizzy.
- **Follow-up:** GP – review Hb in 1-2 weeks, ferritin at 6 weeks; offer debrief with community midwife or consultant at 6-8 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (obstetric registrar / consultant):**
  - Any ongoing bleeding >1000mL despite initial uterotonics
  - Haemodynamic instability (HR>120, SBP<90, shock index >1)
  - Abnormal clotting (PT/APTT >1.5× control, fibrinogen <2g/L)
  - Suspected uterine rupture or inversion
  - Criteria for massive transfusion (active bleeding + shock)
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - GCS <13, respiratory distress, SpO2 <90% on high-flow O2
  - Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest (obstetric cardiac arrest drill – left lateral tilt, prepare for perimortem CS)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2: >6 (or any single parameter red) → immediate clinical review
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Urine output <0.5mL/kg/h for >2h despite fluid resuscitation
